title: Should data centers pay a carbon tax?
gist: Yglesias argues most anti-data-center hype (water, electricity prices, local tax) is overblown, but their carbon emissions are a legitimate issue; the rational response is to accept higher optimal global emissions because AI is economically useful, and to revive carbon pricing by levying it specifically on data centers - a politically novel way to tax a sector the public already dislikes without any consumer-facing price.

insights.claim: Most anti-data-center complaints about water scarcity, electricity prices, and local tax revenue are overblown; there is plenty of water for data centers, reasonable policy keeps them from raising electricity prices, and the local tax revenue they generate is fundamentally valuable.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Local opposition to data-center buildout is largely sentiment, not substance - a headwind that is politically loud but economically weak and likely to be overcome where policy is sane.
insights.quote: There's plenty of water for data centers, with reasonable policy data centers don't drive up electricity prices, and the local tax revenue generated by data centers is fundamentally valuable.
insights.locator: An intelligent and sophisticated minority of people

insights.claim: The standard practice of cities and states writing growth-hostile tax codes and then patching them with data-center tax abatements creates the appearance of corruption even though the abatements are designed to produce a revenue-positive outcome.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: The political risk to data-center economics comes from optics of corruption around abatements, not real fiscal harm - a reform-fixable problem, not a structural one.
insights.quote: standard practice is for cities and states to write bad tax codes that are counterproductive for growth and revenue and then patch them with lots of abatements
insights.locator: One is the tax abatements that data centers receive

insights.claim: Data center carbon emissions are not large compared to emissions from agriculture or industry, so they are not a unique environmental hazard - but that fact alone does not dispose of the climate objection.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Framing data centers as an unprecedented climate threat is rhetorically misleading; the real argument is about marginal load against fixed targets, not absolute scale.
insights.quote: It's not like data centers are some unique environmental hazard the likes of which the world has never seen.

themes.theme: Why fixed climate targets break under AI demand

insights.claim: Reasoning backwards from a 1.5 or 2 degree Celsius temperature target to national and local emissions budgets makes any large new source of energy demand, such as AI, deeply inconvenient because prior 100%-clean-electricity plans assumed lower total demand.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Institutions locked into legacy decarbonization PDFs have a structural incentive to fight AI load growth rather than re-baseline - watch for target-driven regulatory resistance to compute buildout.
insights.quote: if you managed to generate all the clean electricity your PDF promised but now electricity demand is higher than you predicted, you're going to fall short of the 100 percent target
insights.locator: The problem is that if you start with a temperature target

insights.claim: Inventing a new economically useful way to consume electricity rationally raises the globally optimal level of greenhouse-gas emissions and total warming, rather than implying the new use should be blocked.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A first-principles case that AI load justifies more total emissions and energy buildout, not less - bullish for power-sector capacity (gas, nuclear, geothermal, renewables) over emissions-cap orthodoxy.
insights.quote: inventing a new economically useful way to consume electricity simply means that the globally optimal level of greenhouse-gas emissions and total warming has gone up

insights.claim: Standing up a large new electricity load quickly favors natural gas, and with state-of-the-art combined-cycle turbines in short supply it can make sense to deploy less-efficient turbines, because even heavily-renewable buildouts divert land, transmission, and construction capacity from displacing existing fossil use.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Speed-of-deployment economics structurally favor gas turbines for near-term AI load - a tailwind for gas-turbine OEMs and gas-fired generation despite green preferences.
insights.quote: the fastest and cheapest way to stand up the N.C.S. would be to use plenty of natural gas. And with state-of-the-art highly efficient combined-cycle turbines in short supply, it would still make sense to use plenty of less efficient turbines
insights.locator: Suppose someone invented a National Cancer Shield

insights.claim: The AI buildout's pace and rising electricity demand is premised on investors believing AI is economically valuable enough to make money, so the demand surge is itself evidence the technology has real value.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Treat the magnitude of capex/electricity demand as a revealed-preference signal of expected AI returns, not just hype.
insights.quote: The whole pace of construction and rising electricity demand is premised on the fact that investors think they're going to make money.
insights.locator: L.L.M.s, of course, do not cure cancer

insights.claim: Much of the 'AI bubble' narrative is motivated reasoning by climate advocates, because declaring AI economically worthless is the only way to defend fixed climate targets without either accepting more warming or openly asking people to sacrifice useful technology for the sake of polar bears.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Discount bubble claims that originate from climate-target defenders; the bubble framing can be ideologically driven rather than a sober capex assessment.
insights.quote: the most expedient way to defend the targets is to insist that the demand is just a big mistake, that actually this isn't economically useful at all
insights.locator: Given that rising A.I.-related electricity demand is bad news

themes.theme: Global distribution of AI's climate cost

insights.claim: Bernie Sanders proposed a bill giving the public a 50% ownership stake in the largest American AI companies to ensure the trillions created by AI benefit all Americans and to block oligarch decisions harmful to the public.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A sitting senator is floating partial nationalization of frontier AI labs - a tail political risk to AI-lab equity value worth tracking even if unlikely to pass.
insights.quote: I will soon be introducing a bill to give the public a 50% ownership stake in the largest AI companies in America.
insights.locator: I was, however, struck by Bernie Sanders's proposal

insights.claim: Domestic political action can force AI's gains to be shared within a country, but the warming costs fall on the entire world and disproportionately on tropical populations like Nigeria and Bangladesh who have no mechanism to capture AI's benefits.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: The unaddressed externality is cross-border, not domestic - any credible climate-AI policy fix has to be international, which is far harder and likely to lag.
insights.quote: while you can imagine democratic political action forcing the gains to be broadly shared domestically, it's hard to imagine that approach will work for the global poor
insights.locator: Clearly, “a small number of early investors reap all the gains”

themes.theme: Clean energy abundance and the data-center carbon tax

insights.claim: The right climate response is a politics of clean energy abundance that accelerates utility-scale renewables plus game-changing investment in geothermal and nuclear, rather than sloganeering that 'clean is cheap.'
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Reinforces the energy-abundance thesis favoring nuclear and geothermal scale-up as the durable supply-side answer to AI load.
insights.quote: a politics of genuinely doing everything in our power to accelerate the deployment of utility-scale renewables and to facilitate potentially game-changing investments in geothermal and nuclear power

insights.claim: A carbon tax levied specifically on data centers could revive carbon pricing because it imposes no consumer-facing price and burdens only a sector the public already views negatively, sidestepping the usual voter hostility to carbon pricing.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A concrete, politically viable carbon-pricing vector aimed at data centers - a regulatory cost overhang to watch for the entire hyperscale compute sector.
insights.quote: what if we … made data centers pay a carbon tax?

insights.claim: Because the US dominates the tech industry, a data-center carbon tax could be paired with mechanisms penalizing American companies that use foreign non-priced data centers, pressuring the rest of the world to adopt carbon pricing.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A US data-center carbon tax with extraterritorial reach would raise the cost of offshoring compute and could become a de facto global compute-carbon standard.
insights.quote: we could layer on mechanisms to penalize American companies that utilize foreign non-priced data centers, thereby encouraging the whole world to level up
insights.locator: And then, because the United States is such a dominant player
